content: More of a general mailing list question: how do you follow along with a patch after it gets posted? Or at what point does it get merged into rust-next

For example, this patch: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20230628174333.6eb827ae.gary@garyguo.net/T/#m18a4b428e2b8d81c925539e51b6b6606c5610189. It's pretty small, got three Reviewed-bys, and seems complete. What is the next step? Does the author do something, or does Miguel (or someone else) pick when to apply it?

Ha, that's my and Bjorn's patch ;-) Usually the process is waiting for the maintainers to pick it up, Linux kernel does have a 9 or 10 week cycles, so sometimes it might be a long time.

And usually when the maintainer takes the patch, an email will be sent: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CANiq72nbRDp5Ei0dzkKZJw16CzO6iXwXaBZUigz7qKdjBn9ypQ@mail.gmail.com/
I had seen some of the emails but didn't realize they were on a cycle. Is there no indicator of "approved for next pull cycle as-is" vs. "author has a few small changes to make" then?

As a kernel outsider it seems like applying them to the next branch frequently would be better - then others can build off it or adjust as needed, more frequent than the kernel release cycle. If you're working on a patch that requires another (unmerged) patch series, do you typically just manually pull that patch into your working tree then?

Well, if changes are needed, most of time maintainers will just reply early and say it. However, maintainers are human beings as well, so it's possible sometimes a patch is missed. And some maintainers' style is doing the small changes themselves.
Here is the doc for development and cycles: https://docs.kernel.org/process/2.Process.html?highlight=development+cycle
Super understandable. I guess that is the point where something like jira or gerrit would be helpful
Thanks for the link, and for the explanation

Boqun Feng said:

There is https://patchwork.kernel.org/ for some subsystems

That's quite cool. How hard would it be to add rust? Not that the mailing list is so busy it's tough to keep up, but having the overview would be nice

Trevor Gross said:

I had seen some of the emails but didn't realize they were on a cycle. Is there no indicator of "approved for next pull cycle as-is" vs. "author has a few small changes to make" then?

Miguel will post a message when he applies it to the rust-next tree, which is with high probability what will be merged into the next kernel release candidate. It looks like this

In general, figuring out if and when patches were applied is my biggest gripe with the kernel email list workflow.
I guess there is always git log --grep on upstream repository

Andreas Hindborg said:

Miguel will post a message when he applies it to the rust-next tree, which is with high probability what will be merged into the next kernel release candidate.

(just to be clear I have no specific need for this patch, I just found a recent random one as an example)

Andreas Hindborg said:

In general, figuring out if and when patches were applied is my biggest gripe with the kernel email list workflow.

This is kind of part of what I am trying to figure out. It seems like there is no easy way to see at a glance whether a patch is "waiting on author", "waiting on review", "will be merged", and "merged". "merged" is pretty clear like in the link you sent - but I'm kind of surprised that patchwork doesn't have a way for a maintainer to set one of these four states.

I can't imagine doing my day-to-day work if I had to read an entire MR to guess whether it was done or not :smile: but maybe that's just a misunderstanding as a result of being spoiled by github & gitlab

Trevor Gross said:

I can't imagine doing my day-to-day work if I had to read an entire MR to guess whether it was done or not :smile: but maybe that's just a misunderstanding as a result of being spoiled by github & gitlab

git log --grep is actually quite fast. Lets say you have this patch and you want to know if it is applied:

time git log --grep "rust: macros: replace" --oneline rust/rust-next
52b7bb46ae79 rust: macros: replace Self with the concrete type in #[pin_data]
git log --grep "rust: macros: replace" --oneline rust/rust-next  7.84s user 0.11s system 99% cpu 7.956 total

time git log --grep "rust: macros: replace" --oneline upstream/master
git log --grep "rust: macros: replace" --oneline upstream/master  7.82s user 0.15s system 99% cpu 8.014 total

It took me 14 seconds to figure out that it is staged in rust-next.

Lore also has a really good search function with an expressive query language. It is quite easy to find email threads for specific changes with that.
By "done" I meant ready for merge but not applied

Trevor Gross said:

By "done" I meant ready for merge but not applied

Well, usually people will merge something if they think it's ready, before that probably they haven't taken a careful look at the patchset, so it's not ready in that sense.
